hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The replacement string is 'hi=int:3', which represents the literal output for an argument 'hi' with an integer default value of 3, formatted without a type prefix because the type annotation was omitted.

The exact string is 'extra_args=['. This is the literal starting portion of a rendered output line that includes a list of unknown command line tokens (extra args), followed by a comma-separated list of those tokens and a closing bracket.

The number '123' is the specific example of an invalid value provided in the context of a Claude (or similar lexer-based) error for a Literal type constraint, where non-5-digit strings do not match the expected set.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check the 'configure' section for the 'long_arg_name' mapping
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The codebase uses a specific configuration object or configuration list (often a subclass like 'subcommand_config' or passed via 'configure' flags) where the actual argument names are overridden by keys named 'long_arg_name' before the standard 'name' field is used for output rendering.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to the usage_error_categories enum definition
context_pointers.actual_meaning: This field refers to a hardcoded list of valid error category strings (e.g., 'usage', 'file_not_found') defined in an enum or configuration file, explicitly excluding certain types like 'invalid_type_configuration' which should fall into a different handler.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that — check the codebase or the existing test cases for the exact behavior.

feature01_typed_scalar.json.description: Declare a single argument annotated with a scalar type and an inline default value. When the command line does not supply the flag, the parsed value equals the declared default. When the flag is supplied, the parsed value is the string token taken from the command line. The rendered output lists the field name and its typed value.

feature02_untyped_argument.json.description: Arguments may be declared with only a default value and no type annotation, or added at configuration time with no annotation at all. An untyped argument keeps its default when the flag is absent; when supplied on the command line its value is taken verbatim as text (no numeric conversion is inferred). A configuration-time flag with no backing default simply captures the supplied text.

feature03_string_annotation.json.description: Type annotations may be provided as strings (forward references) rather than as live type objects; they are resolved to the same behavior. A scalar string annotation drives numeric conversion and a parameterized collection string annotation drives element-wise conversion of a whitespace-separated list. Defaults apply when flags are absent.

feature04_required_arguments.json.description: An annotated argument with no default value is required. If any required argument is missing from the command line the parse fails with a usage error. When every required argument is supplied the parse succeeds and the values are converted according to their annotations.

feature05_implicit_bool.json.description: A boolean argument with a default value becomes a no-argument flag whose presence flips it to the opposite of its default: a field defaulting to true reads as false when the flag is given, and a field defaulting to false reads as true when the flag is given. When neither flag is present both keep their declared defaults.

feature06_explicit_bool.json.description: In explicit-boolean mode a boolean argument expects a literal truth value as its argument. Truthy spellings (full word, single letter, or one) parse to true and falsy spellings (full word, single letter, or zero) parse to false, in any capitalization. Omitting the value after the flag, or omitting a required boolean entirely, is a usage error.

feature07_literal_choices.json.description: A literal-typed argument restricts the accepted values to a fixed set of allowed options and converts the chosen value to the type of the matching option (so a string option stays text, a numeric option becomes a number, and a boolean option becomes a boolean). Supplying a value outside the allowed set is a usage error. The default is used when the flag is absent.

feature08_optional_types.json.description: An optional-typed argument defaults to an empty (none) value and, when supplied, converts the command-line text to the inner type. Several optional arguments of different inner types can coexist; each is converted independently.

feature09_list_arguments.json.description: A list-typed argument consumes all whitespace-separated tokens following its flag and converts each element to the declared element type (bare list keeps strings, and typed lists convert to integers, floats, or booleans). Element order is preserved.

cases.expected_output:
list_=[str:I, str:was, str:wondering]
list_bool=[bool:True, bool:False]
list_int=[int:0, int:1, int:2]
list_str=[str:a, str:bee, str:cd, str:ee]

feature10_set_arguments.json.description: A set-typed argument consumes the whitespace-separated tokens after its flag, converts each to the declared element type, and collapses duplicate values into a unique collection (membership only, order not significant).

cases.expected_output:
set_int={int:1, int:2, int:3}
set_str={str:a, str:bee, str:cd}

feature11_tuple_arguments.json.description: A tuple-typed argument fixes the number and per-position types of its elements. A bare tuple keeps an arbitrary number of string elements; a single-element typed tuple yields one converted element; a homogeneous ellipsis tuple converts an arbitrary number of elements to one type; and a fully enumerated tuple converts each position to its own declared type.

cases.expected_output:
tup_bool=(bool:False, bool:True, bool:False, bool:True, bool:True)
tup_int=(int:2, int:3, int:4)

feature12_tuple_errors.json.description: A typed tuple enforces both the count and the per-position types of its elements. Supplying an element that cannot be converted to its declared type, supplying the wrong number of elements, or supplying elements in an order that violates the position types, all fail with a usage error. Declaring an empty tuple type is rejected up front as an invalid type configuration.

feature13_noncollection_default.json.description: If a collection-typed argument is given a default that is not itself a collection (for example an empty/none value or a bare scalar), and the flag is not supplied on the command line, the declared default value is returned unchanged without any collection conversion.

feature14_path_types.json.description: A filesystem-path type is supported as a scalar element type and inside optional, list, set, and tuple containers. Each supplied token is wrapped into a path value; collections of paths preserve their respective semantics (list ordered, set unique, tuple fixed-length).

feature15_union_requires_converter.json.description: A general union of two or more concrete types (other than an optional, i.e. a type combined with none) cannot be parsed automatically because the parser cannot know which member type to convert to. Declaring such a union without supplying an explicit conversion function is rejected as an invalid type configuration.

feature16_as_dict.json.description: After parsing, the full set of resolved arguments can be retrieved as a name-to-value mapping. The mapping includes typed fields, untyped fields, optional fields (with their none defaults), and collection fields, as well as arguments introduced under a renamed destination at configuration time.

cases.expected_output:
a=str:hi
b=int:1
c=bool:True
d=(str:hi, str:bob)
e=none
f={int:1}

feature17_from_dict.json.description: A parser can be populated directly from a name-to-value mapping instead of from the command line, reproducing the same retrievable arguments. If the mapping omits a required argument that has no default, population fails with an invalid-request error.

cases.expected_output:
a=str:hi
b=int:1
c=bool:True
d=(str:a, str:b)
e=int:7
f={int:1}

feature18_known_only.json.description: In known-only mode the parser consumes the arguments it recognizes and collects every unrecognized command-line token, in order, into a separate extra-arguments list rather than failing. Recognized arguments are still converted normally.

feature19_underscores_to_dashes.json.description: When underscore-to-dash conversion is enabled, multi-word flag names are spelled with dashes on the command line instead of underscores, while the resolved attribute names keep their underscore form. This applies to annotated fields and to flags registered at configuration time.

feature20_config_files.json.description: Arguments can be loaded from one or more configuration files, each containing command-line-style flag tokens. Command-line arguments override config values; among multiple config files, later files override earlier ones. Config content supports shell-style comments and quoted multi-word values.

feature21_config_errors.json.description: Configuration-file handling fails cleanly: pointing at a path that does not exist raises a file-not-found error; content that cannot be parsed as valid flag tokens raises a usage error; and a required argument that is still not provided by any config file or the command line raises a usage error.

feature22_actions.json.description: Configuration-time arguments can opt into accumulation behaviors: an append action collects one converted value per occurrence onto the list default; an append-constant action appends a fixed constant per occurrence; a count action tallies the number of occurrences onto a numeric default; and an extend action flattens multiple multi-valued occurrences into the default list.

feature23_subparsers.json.description: A parser can register named subcommands, each with its own arguments. Selecting a subcommand on the command line activates only that subcommand's arguments; the top-level arguments remain available before the subcommand token. When no subcommand is selected, only the top-level arguments are present. A subcommand's own arguments are unavailable unless that subcommand is chosen.

feature24_override_inferred.json.description: Re-declaring an annotated field at configuration time lets the developer override the inferred settings: a supplied default replaces the inferred one, and a supplied conversion type replaces the inferred type (so a field annotated as numeric can be forced to stay text). Flags may use a single dash, double dash, or both as aliases.

feature25_lifecycle_errors.json.description: The parser enforces its usage lifecycle. Parsing the command line more than once on the same parser instance is an invalid request, and adding a new argument after the parser has finished initializing is likewise an invalid request.

feature26_positional.json.description: An argument can be registered as a positional (no leading dash) at configuration time while still drawing its type from the matching annotated field, so the positional token is converted to the declared type. Positional names are never rewritten by underscore-to-dash conversion.

_constraints.input_condition: User declares an argument via the 'arguments' list.
_constraints.output_rule: Each argument must have a 'name' string. 'type' is optional (defaults to string/implied). 'default' is optional. Omitting 'type' implies 'str'. Omitting 'default' implies required.

_constraints.input_condition: An argument has a non-string 'default' (e.g., int 3) but no 'type' annotation.
_constraints.output_rule: If the flag is absent, render as <name>=<type:default_value> using the Python type of the default (str:int, str:float).

_constraints.input_condition: An untyped or implicitly typed argument is supplied on the command line.
_constraints.output_rule: The command line token is converted verbatim as a string. No numeric conversion occurs even if the default was a number.
Output lines MUST be sorted alphabetically by field name.

_constraints.input_condition: A collection-typed argument (List/Set/Tuple/Opt) has a non-collection 'default' (e.g., null or scalar 3) and the flag is absent.
_constraints.output_rule: Render the declared default value exactly as provided (typed based on its type), NOT as an empty collection.

_constraints.input_condition: An argument resolves to None (either explicitly null default or null inside 'from_dict' for Optional).
_constraints.output_rule: Render as 'name=none' without a type prefix.

_constraints.input_condition: Subcommands or 'known_only=false' (implied by default behavior of extra capture) produce unknown tokens.
_constraints.output_rule: Render section as 'extra_args=[<token1>, <token2>, ...]' separated by commas and spaces.

_constraints.input_condition: The resolved value is a List, Set, or Tuple.
_constraints.output_rule: (List: [t0, ...], Set: {t0, ...}, Tuple: (t0, ...)). Elements are type-tagged. Set elements MUST be sorted lexicographically for determinism.

_constraints.input_condition: Any parse, config, or request failure occurs.
_constraints.output_rule: Output exactly one line: 'error=<type>'. Forbidden types: usage, file_not_found, invalid_type_configuration, invalid_request.

_constraints.input_condition: Missing required argument, invalid choice for Literal, tuple arity mismatch, type conversion failure.
Union of concrete types (e.g., Union[int, float]) or empty tuple (Tuple[()]) declared.
_constraints.output_rule: Output 'error=invalid_type_configuration' (for Union) or runtime catch for Tuple(()).

_constraints.input_condition: Boolean type with default NO value (flag) where argTrue has default True, argFalse has default False.
_constraints.output_rule: Providing the flag flips the value: True->False, False->True.
Requires argument value. Truthy tokens (True, true, False, false, 1, 0, etc.) map accordingly. No value provided on flag = usage error.

_constraints.input_condition: Value supplied for Literal type is not in the allowed set.

_constraints.output_rule: Tokens are converted as string paths. Formatting matches standard Output Contract (e.g., path:/path).

_constraints.input_condition: Population via 'from_dict' omits a required field (type=..., no default).

_constraints.output_rule: Introduce new flags' names, override 'type', or change 'action' logic (implied). 'action' is the operational key.

_constraints.input_condition: 'configure' uses 'flags' list with different dash names vs declared names.
_constraints.output_rule: Unknown dependency: Mapping retrieves values by 'long_arg_name' (configured) or 'name' (declared). Output uses configured name.
